title: Validity of DFT+U band gaps in all its known functional forms
authors: Andrew C. Burgess, David D. O'Regan
abstract: The Density Functional Theory plus Hubbard $U$ (DFT+$U$) technique is one of the most widely used tools by condensed matter physicists and solid state chemists for the simulation of transition-metal and lanthanide bearing crystals, and increasingly of much more diverse chemistries. Although often synonymous with the corrective functionals of Dudarev et al. and Liechtenstein et al., there exists a wide variety of DFT+$U$-type functionals ready to be utilized, and no doubt yet to be developed. Since the earliest days, the gap in the DFT+$U$ single-particle eigenspectrum has been associated with the fundamental band gap, and the method has typically found more success for spectra than for total-energy derived properties. There has been some doubt, however, as to the conceptual validity of this association. Here, extending findings from recent years regarding local and semi-local functionals, we prove that the DFT+$U$ eigenspectrum gap is indeed valid, in the sense that it matches its own fundamental gap calculated using total-energy differences. This is true for pristine periodic systems with converged $k$-point sampling but not, however, for defective ones or isolated systems. We show that bandgap validity for solids holds in the presence of pseudopotentials and PAW potentials, when using hybrid functionals, and in DFT+$U$(+$J$) irrespective of the level of subspace projection onto the band-edge states. We survey every DFT+$U$-type functional known to have been published to date, within a unified notation. We verify analytically under which conditions the eigenvalue gap equals its fundamental gap for each functional, and analyze its effect on total energies and gaps for the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.
summary: This paper provides a formal theoretical proof that the eigenvalue gap in DFT+U and hybrid functional calculations accurately represents the fundamental band gap for periodic solids. By analyzing the scaling of functional derivatives in the thermodynamic limit, the authors show that the derivative discontinuity term vanishes in the bulk. This resolves long-standing doubts about the conceptual validity of using single-particle spectra as a proxy for total-energy-derived gaps. However, they also clarify that this equivalence breaks down for molecules and defective systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether the single-particle eigenspectrum gap (eigenvalue gap) in DFT+U and hybrid functionals is a theoretically valid proxy for the fundamental band gap.
main_result: The authors prove that for pristine periodic crystals in the thermodynamic limit, the DFT+U eigenvalue gap exactly matches the fundamental gap, a result that holds across various functional forms and projection operators.
method: A rigorous analytical derivation using the Generalized Kohn-Sham (GKS) framework, applying Janak's theorem and scaling analysis of functional derivatives with respect to electron count.
model_or_system: The study focuses on pristine, infinite periodic crystals (solids) in the thermodynamic limit, including transition-metal and lanthanide-bearing crystals and the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.
key_observables: Fundamental band gap (energy difference between electron addition and removal), single-particle eigenspectrum gap (eigenvalue gap), and total energies.
important_parameters: Hubbard U and Hund's J parameters, effective Hubbard parameter Ueff, and subspace projection operators.
main_assumptions: The validity is strictly limited to pristine periodic systems with converged k-point sampling; it does not apply to isolated molecules or defective solids.
figures_summary: Figure 1 is a logarithmic scale bar chart showing the widespread utility of the DFT+U method by displaying Google Scholar hits for various DFT software packages.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the energy functional and GKS Hamiltonian, proceeds to an analytical proof of the eigenvalue gap validity in the bulk limit, extends the proof to various functional forms and projection methods, and concludes by discussing the limitations regarding defective and isolated systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The number of Google Scholar hits [103], on a log- arithmic scale, for a range of DFT packages that implement it [104–131]. The search was conducted by searching the name of the DFT package in conjunction with DFT+U.

FIG. 2. DFT+U correction to the total energy of the spin polarized and non-spin polarised hydrogen lattice at the in- finite separation limit upon non-self consistent application of each DFT+U-type functional. The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Seo’s DFT+U functional yields a negative en- ergetic correction for both the spin polarized and non-spin polarized hydrogen lattices of -2 eV and -1 eV, respectively.

FIG. 3. Correction to the bandgap of the spin polarized hy- drogen lattice at the infinite separation limit upon non-self consistent application of each DFT+U-type functional. The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Two DFT+U functionals, namely Anisimov ’91 and BLOR, reduce the Kohn Sham gap by 5 eV and 2 eV, respectively, while most other functionals increase the Kohn Sham gap of this system.

FIG. 4. Correction to the bandgap of the non-spin polarized hydrogen lattice at the infinite separation limit upon non-self consistent application of each DFT+U-type functional. The Hubbard U, Hund’s J and intersite V parameters were set at 5 eV, 1 eV and 0 eV, respectively. Most DFT+U functionals fail to modify the Kohn Sham gap of this system.

FIG. 5. Dudarev’s DFT+U correction to the bandgap ∆σ GKS as a function of the spin-resolved subspace occupancy eigenvalues of a d-orbital subspace under octahedral or tetrahedral crystal field splitting. Under octahedral or tetrahedral crystal field splitting, in the absence of further symmetry breaking, there will be two eigenspaces (of dimensions 2 and 3) of the d-orbital spin-resolved subspace occupancy matrix, the eigenvalues of which we label λσ eg and λσ t2g.
